intro:
In Scripture a table is never merely furniture; to share a table is to share life. A meal seals covenants, expresses welcome, and binds host and guest in fellowship. So when the Bible speaks of the LORD's table, it speaks of God hosting his people — feeding them, drawing them near, and pledging communion with them.

The image is given liturgical form in the tabernacle, where God commands a golden table bearing the Bread of the Presence — twelve loaves set out continually before him (Exodus 25:23–30; Leviticus 24:5–9). Israel's life is lived, as it were, at God's table. At Sinai the elders ascend and "beheld God, and ate and drank" in his presence (Exodus 24:11), a covenant meal of astonishing intimacy. The Psalmist makes it personal: "You prepare a table before me in the presence of my enemies" (Psalm 23:5) — the LORD as gracious host even amid danger. Wisdom likewise spreads a table and invites the simple to eat (Proverbs 9:1–5), while the prophets foresee a final banquet: "On this mountain the LORD of hosts will make for all peoples a feast of rich food" (Isaiah 25:6). The table can also be profaned — Malachi rebukes priests for despising "the table of the LORD" with polluted offerings (Malachi 1:7).

Jesus takes up the image as a hallmark of his ministry, scandalously eating with tax collectors and sinners (Luke 5:29–32) to embody the welcome of God. At the Last Supper he gives the table its deepest meaning, and promises his disciples that they "may eat and drink at my table in my kingdom" (Luke 22:30). After the resurrection he is known to the Emmaus disciples "in the breaking of the bread" (Luke 24:30–31). Paul therefore calls the Supper sharing in "the table of the Lord," a fellowship so real that it cannot be combined with "the table of demons" (1 Corinthians 10:21).

The motif reaches its consummation at the end. The risen Christ stands at the door and promises, "I will come in to him and eat with him, and he with me" (Revelation 3:20), and history closes with "the marriage supper of the Lamb" (Revelation 19:9). From the bread of the Presence to the wedding feast, the table traces the whole purpose of redemption: that God would gather a people to sit and eat in his presence forever.
